speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your arrival at Auschwitz and moving into the camp that had previously been the gypsy camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were describing the barracks. Please go ahead with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was the-- it was called the Zigeunerlager. And before I knew, I was in-- in one of these barracks-- not with those people that I knew, but it was-- again, you know, they packed in x amount of people into those barracks. And before I knew, I-- I found out that my uncle is there already-- Uncle Max-- and that he is already in-- in Auschwitz. But he came from way in the east, in Poland. And being that he was a very good-hearted fella-- he was a real good uncle. If one needs an uncle, he is the guy to-- to really know. And I-- I went to that barrack. Somebody told me where his barrack is. And so I went there. And they wouldn't let me in. They said, Mr. Guterman sleeps now. He is now resting. Well, he was the cook for the-- for the Lagermeister, the-- the guy who-- who was-- the guy who was really in charge of-- of this whole barrack there. And so they-- he let him have some benefits. And one of the benefits was that he was able to lay down and sleep in the afternoon. Maybe he had to get up very early every morning and cook or whatever. It could have been. Well, I said to the man that I don't want to see Mr. Guterman. Just tell me where is he sleeping. So he showed me, you know, and this and this outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I did-- we used to have, as kids, you know, being-- we would-- we would whistle to one another. And I-- I whistled. I went to the wall and I whistled, calling Uncle Max. And I don't know. Three minutes later-- two or three minutes later, there comes Max and looks around the whole building. There was some kind of a whistle that he recognized.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, that was a big thing. He right away took us in. And I was there with a friend of mine, Sid [? Foreman, ?] who I was five and a half years together in camps. And he fed us, this and he gave us bread, and he did everything in the world to help us. And so-- this was, of course, a good thing, that we met him. But, you know, the ovens went on constantly. And they would burn people there, and gas 'em first. And so that was Auschwitz. About-- I think it was about two months most that I was there. And-- and they said, no, they got to send us out. And my uncle said, out of here, out of here. They are burning people here. They are gassing people here. I don't want you to stay. And he was the one that pushed me to really get in into a-- into a group to be sent to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go ahead with that, I want to ask about your mother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they went at Auschwitz, where they lived [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the-- with the women's camp, you know? They didn't have the men and the women together. So they-- they died in-- in Auschwitz. They were gassed and-- and burned in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately upon arriving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I understand they were there a few weeks-- because I never saw them again. I did never see them again. So this is all I can say. What I heard from Uncle Max and what I heard from some other people-- said, well, yeah, your mother, she was there, and that-- that-- that's what had happened. That's all I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a day at Auschwitz? Start in the morning and describe an average day.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, Auschwitz was, in fact, comparatively to other camps, not such a bad camp if you were able to live there. The only problem in Auschwitz was if they ran out of bodies to kill, if they ran out of people to kill, the danger was they're gonna catch you sooner or later-- because if they need 200 people to gas, and the transport didn't come in, they'll go into the camp and-- and make a razzia, what we called, and-- and say, here, you go this way. And-- and you're gonna go to a camp. But they will have some kind of excuse. And before you know it, you-- you got in into the gas chamber. You're gonna take a shower. Instead of water, the gas came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was because there was-- the food was OK. They gave you enough to live on. But the fact that we-- there was no continuity, you know? There's no work. They didn't put you into a class of working area, where you knew everybody, you have to go to here. You got up in the morning. You had to stay Appell, you know? And they would count the people, and da, da, da, and-- and then clean up the camp. And that's how we would do, 'cause nothing-- nothing special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a while, they would grab a few people out of the camp and let them do some things. But it wasn't anything that was continuous. So we knew that Auschwitz is not going to be a place we're gonna stay. It's something that you just passed by, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I said, I went, and my friend went together with me, Sid [? Foreman. ?] He is-- he lives now in Miami. But that's-- we arrived then, of course, in Dachau [INAUDIBLE]. But when you were still at Auschwitz-- tell me a little bit more about that. What did you eat at Auschwitz? What type of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Auschwitz, they would give you, you know, the soldier-- for the military, they had these breads. We had-- to start off with, I think the first few days, they gave us-- like, two people a bread. I mean, it was plenty to eat-- and good, fat, big-- not fat, but thick soup. They fed you well in Auschwitz. And that was it. [LAUGHS] And get up in the morning and do again the same thing. This is how it was, and until they would organize the groups to be sent out. If you had the good group to really go out to work, like I did-- I was lucky. It could have been a-- a group that they said, we need-- we need 200 people to-- voom. Instead of a left turn, they make a right turn, and that's it. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were lined up for selections, you had no idea what you were being selected for?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less no. They would say, yeah, they need from 18 to 22. I was always in the-- in the group. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When they-- when they-- when they-- when they were talking about age, I always looked a little older, because I told the guy, you know, when-- when they looked up and the muscles when you came into Auschwitz-- how old are you? 18. 18 is always the best age. [LAUGHS] So I was able to kind of go through it because of that. So they never really said-- they said, yeah, they're looking for a group that is gonna go to Munich, Berlin, [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't know themselves what they were doing at this point. They had to get a group together of 200 people, send them out where the trains are, and then somebody else will take over, and will take those people and go on with them to some cities in Germany by train. Were people in your groups ever sent to the gas chambers that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I never saw them again. You [LAUGHS] never know, you know? I mean, there's some people and-- and Auschwitz, big turnover. Big, big turnover. As I said, I-- I must-- I must say that probably, when we came from Lodz to-- to-- Auschwitz, I would say 60%, 70% of the people went into the gas chamber. So we didn't know who went. And then each of these camps, you know, each of these barracks, you would know where those people were. I was-- I met a-- a friend of mine who was related to us. I went to London to see him this last summer. And I met him. Last time I saw him was in Auschwitz. And I knew that his father was caught in one of those razzias because Uncle Max told me. He said, you know, this-- this guy and-- [? Kutner ?] was his name. They caught him and he-- so he didn't notice. He was in some other place and they got him-- You-- you know, when they-- you-- you have-- you must visualize that there was a lot of-- if you look back, it's like confusion. But it wasn't. They knew exactly what they were doing. But what they-- what they were doing is some people, they knew they need 200 people to burn. They took 200 people. They didn't care who it was, but they took-- they brought 200 people to the gas chamber. So-- now that I found out about him-- and, you know, he arrived-- we talked on the telephone. This is, of course, after almost 50 years. And he said, yeah, I never know what my-- what happened to my father. I don't know. I say, your father-- I say, your father was-- was-- was killed in-- in Auschwitz. That's what Uncle Max told me and [INAUDIBLE]. He-- of course, he was crying and this. He never knew. He never knew where his father was after 50 years [INAUDIBLE]. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I met a lot of-- I met a lot of people in-- in Auschwitz. And you make also friends when you work with somebody. The same day, or two days later, three days later, you make friends. So this is how it was. But I would say that out of the transports coming in from Lodz-- I would say about 60%, 70% of the people died in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of them, of course, died before they even were sent out of the ghetto. The ghetto-- a lot of people died in the ghetto. But once you came into Auschwitz, that's where it was, the elimination. Oh, children right away was, you know-- they-- they killed the kids right away. Did you know where the-- the gas ovens and the crematoriums were located? Could you see them and see the smoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, yes. At the time, we could see the smoke. And they said they're burning people. So, you know, you always visualize if a-- if a person is 17, 18, 19, or 20 years old, he thinks about they're-- they're taking people, they-- 60, 70-year-old person. And they're gonna maybe get rid of him, or maybe a person that cannot walk, cannot-- you know? But never take any human beings who are capable of working and capable of thinking, some intelligent people. You must understand, the Germans they took first the intelligentsia. They were the doctors and the lawyers and the-- all this. This was the first job, to eliminate them, because they were dangerous to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your uncle, Max. How was it that he was able to survive as well as he did there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] he was a very unusual fellow. He was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess he was working and he was-- he too got in into a ghetto situation, but it was in the eastern part of Poland. And he met this woman and he got married with-- they somehows-- they were able to live there for a while. And he was a very good-hearted fellow. And-- and I guess, you know, as I say, he came into Auschwitz. And as this group came in and they ask who is a cook, he lifts his arm. He says, I'm a cook. In the First World War, he was-- he was peeling potatoes in a-- in a-- you know, in a-- some facilities. So he said he can cook. And he cooked for this guy. And the guy liked him for doing all this, so he kind of protected him in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the guy was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I met him. He was also a-- a-- a person staying in-- in Auschwitz, but for [INAUDIBLE] he was more political.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: A prisoner, yes. He was also a prisoner. But he was the head of that-- of that area where it-- the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about when you left Auschwitz. What-- what was that like the day that you left, and who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went together with my friend, Sid-- Sid [? Foreman. ?] And somehows, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we got there-- oh, well, when we left, the morning, they said they are organizing a group to go. And my uncle said, go. Go, get out of here. Here, they're burning. They're burning people here. They're gassing people. Get out of here. I didn't understand. I was a-- I was too young, too naive to understand. He was there already a few months, maybe. So he [? understand-- ?] he knew what-- what the system is out there. So he said, get out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every group-- you know, sometimes they wanted to have groups of 30-years-olds. I couldn't go. But when they wanted 18, 19, 20, 25, 22, I could always-- and also go maybe with some younger people. But he said, never say that you're under 14. He knew that, you know, young kids, they will eliminate them. And how old were you at that time, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was about 17 years old or 18. 18 years. So we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went there. And they grouped together. And they counted out 200. Yeah, with muscles like this, shoulders like this, this size. Go-- you're going. That was it. And two days later, we arrived in-- in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just, like, you know, one of those cattle trains. And they gave us a piece of bread on the way and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time-- time of year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was summer. It was, like, August '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's talk about your arrival at Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there. And we arrived in-- not far outside of-- not far outside of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Landsberg. Landsberg is a town of about 20,000, 25,000 people. And it was very famous in Germany because they had a very-- the-- they-- they had-- had a jail at the time there. And-- and they jailed Hitler and his comrades for the-- for the deal that they did in 1923 or whatever. And they-- they put him in jail. And that's where he wrote And so in that area-- that whole area there was full of camps. Every 5, 10 kilometers, there was a camp someplace of 2,000 people, or 1,500 people, or whatever. And we were supposed to build a-- a bunker where they're gonna keep ammunition there and build aeroplanes. It's all underground. It was built underground. And that's where they put us up. But when we got there, the-- the-- the camp wasn't finished. You know, people were still working there, building that camp. And-- but finally they finished it, I don't know, after a day. We moved in into the camp. They gave us wooden shoes. They gave us the uniforms. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number there? Were you assigned a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the same number, the [? 95004. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's where it was organized. And they started organizing groups. Each one had to go and work in a certain group. We worked to build this-- this bunker there. It was-- I would say there were at least 25,000 people working there-- at least 25,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In these camps-- I showed you the picture-- we lived in-- in those-- you know, it was like you have a-- you know, here, you put away potatoes for the next year in winter. This is how they build those-- those-- those barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you describe an average day at Dachau from the morning-- starting in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They would wake us up 6 o'clock or 5 o'clock, 5:30, 6:00, depending who was running the camp that day. They would wake us up, go to the bathroom, take a-- a shower. They-- they had water. Ice-cold water. Ice cold. And we would wash there-- or if they didn't have a shower, we wouldn't wash. We wouldn't wash sometimes for six, seven weeks. And get some food. They would give us the piece of bread and some chicory. Instead of coffee, it was chicory. And that was it. And then stay outside until the-- the master, the big guy will come and make sure that we are all out there, so on the Appellplatz. And he would let us-- [INAUDIBLE] was raining and snowing or whatever it was, it didn't matter. He-- they had to count us. They count us again. And then they would go in and have discussions over us and [INAUDIBLE]. Anyways, this is how it happened. We got to work. It was, like-- at the beginning, it was two shifts, 12 hours a shift. So we would work from 7:00 to 7:00. I mean, we would have to walk about a-- an hour, hour and a half, to-- to work. And we would work 12 hours, then come home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we came home, we had to pick up the soup. And then after a while, they changed the-- the idea of having the bread in the morning, so they gave it to you in the evening. The soup and a piece of bread, that's for next day. To give you an idea, as I said before, that we did in Auschwitz, we got two people a bread, and then it became three people a bread. At that time, when the-- about six weeks before the war ended, we got 20 people a bread. 20 people. It was a slice that thick. That was it. And people were dying. People were just dying. I mean, you just didn't have enough strength. We had to carry home every day so many people to the camp because we had to bring 'em back, because they-- they said if 50 people go out on a group to go to work, 50 people must come back dead or alive. We must have them. We must count them in. Otherwise, they thought maybe somebody ran away or stuff like this. We never had 50 people. But we had maybe 200 people, 250 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was really the life going on in Kaufering, which was near by-- near by Landsberg. There were a lot of camps around. There was Lager Eins, Lager Two, Lager Three, Lager Four, Lager-- they had 11 camps. Would you go outside of Dachau to do this work during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We would go out of camp to do the-- outside, yeah. Sometimes they would keep you inside to clean up the camp. But that would have been on a Sunday or a day that they wouldn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any communication or did you see German civilians during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We saw civilians, but we never communicated with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We communicated a little bit with the Wehrmacht. That were the soldiers, the German soldiers. They were-- they were better than the SS or the SA. They were better. We could communicate a little bit. They would say, the war is coming to an end. Don't worry about it, you know? I want to go home to mama also and all this. But not-- not with civilians, no. Were you picking up some German during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I have-- by that time, I already started picking up some German language and stuff like this. I was able to communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the winter months there at Dachau? How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- you know, in Kaufering, it was very cold. We had snow about-- about 2, 3 feet high. It's a very, very cold area. It's Bavarian. In Bavaria, it's the coldest in Germany, and a lot of snow. Sometimes you hit a meter high-- I mean, 3 feet high, 3 and 1/2 foot-high snow. And there were times when we came out-- at one time, I came home from night shift-- you see, after a while, they-- two shifts couldn't make it. They saw that they are losing production. So they made three shifts out of it, which was better. We worked only eight hours a day. We came home from the evening shift in the morning. Everybody's tired. Got to get their soup and go to sleep. Got-- got-- do-- do something. They-- they didn't-- they didn't care because sometimes, they couldn't do the things in the camp because of the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took our whole group. They picked out 40 people out of our group that came back from the night shift and say, you go and pick up the bread. You go walk. We walked for two hours going. And until they loaded the breads and then come back with the breads, it was-- the day was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they counted in the breads, there were two breads missing. I know. We-- we all ate 'em. I mean, we ate 'em during the day. So-- well, they took some people and they punished them, and one was me. Well, I got 25 on my back. And this was part of a-- of a punishment that I had to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know this may be difficult, but please try to explain that more. Under what circumstances did that happen and were you-- were you-- were you restrained? How-- how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came back to camp. And after they had managed to-- we had all managed to get the bread out into the area where they needed, in the kitchen there. And there were two breads missing. So they started looking in the pockets and all this. Show me your pocket. Show me this. They showed-- I must have had some crumb in a pocket. And I was picked, and another fellow. I had to sit on his head. And he [INAUDIBLE] in 25 into him. And then-- fortunately, he broke his-- his-- his whip. And-- and then the other guy was sitting on my head. And I got mine-- that for about six weeks, I couldn't even lay on my back. I was-- I was ruined.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is basically the only thing they did for me. They were nice enough. The-- the-- the manager of the-- of that-- of our camp-- you know, I went there and I told them the story what happened. I-- I said, I didn't do it, you know? So he called one of the Kapos. And he said, when you go back to work tonight, you make sure that he goes into-- we had, like, a hospital. If somebody got injured on the job or stuff like this, they was able to let him rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: For about a week, I didn't have to work. When I got there, the men knew that I was injured because my back was-- was blue. So for about a week. But for six weeks, I couldn't lay on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it that gave you the lashes? A Kapo? A German who-- Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a German-- one of the German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the Germans that did it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. While you were in Dachau, did you hear any news of what was happening in the outside world? What-- the approaching Allies, anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The soldiers, the-- the Wehrmacht, the regular German soldiers-- that's the ones who would communicate with us because as we walked to work, they were the ones that would-- on-- on the sides, they would walk and talk to us a little bit. They would say, the Germans are losing and all this. They wanted to give us a little hope that we are still alive or that we will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop for a tape change, please.
